Home / Glossary / Time Series Analysis
Glossary

What is time series analysis?

Updated 2026-04-21

Time series analysis is the study of data points collected sequentially over time — sales by day, temperature by hour, page views by minute. It decomposes the series into trend, seasonality, and residual components, models the dependency between consecutive observations, and uses the result to forecast future values with an explicit error band.

2 full cycles
Minimum history required for a model to learn seasonality reliably — e.g. 24 months of monthly data for annual seasonality.
Hyndman & Athanasopoulos, Forecasting: Principles and Practice (3rd ed.)
MAPE 5–10%
Industry benchmark for an excellent monthly demand forecast on established products; new products typically land at 15–25%.
Institute of Business Forecasting & Planning — Forecast Accuracy Benchmarks

See also: how to build a 3-month sales forecast · how to compare this quarter vs last quarter · AXIOM vs Looker Studio.

The classic decomposition

Every time series can be split into three pieces:

  • Trend — the long-run direction (growing, shrinking, flat).
  • Seasonality — repeating patterns at a fixed period (weekly, monthly, annual).
  • Residual — what's left after removing trend and seasonality; ideally pure noise.

Looking at the decomposition before modelling tells you whether to use additive (constant seasonality) or multiplicative (seasonality grows with the level) models.

Common model families

  • Naive / seasonal naive — the baseline every other model must beat.
  • Exponential smoothing (ETS) — fast, robust, great for short series with seasonality.
  • ARIMA / SARIMA — captures autocorrelation; needs a stationary series.
  • Prophet — Meta's open-source library; tolerates missing data and holiday effects.
  • Tree-based regressors with lag features — Random Forest / XGBoost on lagged columns; the modern default for tabular forecasting.

The non-negotiable: time-aware holdouts

Never shuffle a time series before splitting. Train on the past, test on the future. AXIOM's predictions tab enforces this by holding out the most recent N periods and reporting MAPE on that window — the only honest accuracy estimate for forecasting.

Frequently asked questions

Is time series the same as regression?

No — regression assumes independent observations. Time series observations are correlated with their own past, which requires different validation and model families.

Do I need to make the series stationary?

ARIMA-family models need stationarity (differencing usually achieves it). Prophet and tree-based models do not.

How do I handle missing dates?

Fill explicit zeros for periods where the count was genuinely zero, and impute or interpolate periods where the data was simply not collected. The two are very different.

Can I forecast a single number?

You can, but you shouldn't. Always report a range — point estimate ± holdout error — so consumers know how much uncertainty to budget for.

What about external regressors like marketing spend?

Useful only if their future values are known at forecast time. Planned spend works; reactive spend that depends on sales does not.

Related terms

Predictive Analytics

Using historical data and statistical or ML models to forecast future outcomes or behaviours.

Data Drift

A change over time in the statistical properties of input data that degrades a model's accuracy.

Descriptive Statistics

Summary measures (mean, median, std, percentiles) that describe the central tendency and spread of a dataset.

Data Cleaning

The process of detecting and correcting errors, inconsistencies, missing values, and duplicates in a dataset before analysis.
